title: The Unrehearsed Answer
story_reference: Based on story-001-the-arrival.md, scene: the Guru asks 'Why?' and the rehearsed speech dissolves on the disciple's tongue
hook: He had rehearsed what to say on the plane. It did not survive the first word the old man spoke.

script.visual: Extreme close-up: an older hand, weathered, opening slowly. Cut to an eye — not soft, not mystical. Clear. Unafraid. Like an X-ray looking back.
script.voiceover: He had expected warmth. Or that vacant gentleness he had seen in the eyes of wellness influencers.

script.visual: Two-shot, low light. Disciple seated opposite the Guru. The Guru's voice quiet, unhurried.
script.voiceover: The old man said only two words. 'You came.' And then, after a long silence: 'Why?'

script.visual: Quick cuts — inside the disciple's head. A plane window. A notebook with bullet points. A mouth rehearsing in a bathroom mirror: 'I am here because I have everything and feel nothing...' Phrases written and crossed out.
script.voiceover: He had rehearsed the speech on the plane. About meaning. About purpose. About the emptiness success could not touch. He had it word-perfect.

script.visual: Back to the two-shot. The disciple opens his mouth. Nothing comes out. Long pause. The Guru does not help him.
script.voiceover: The words dissolved before they reached his tongue.

script.visual: Close on the disciple. Voice cracks. He is not performing. Hands shake.
script.voiceover: 'I don't know what I am. I don't know what binds me. I don't know how it started. I don't know how to be free. I don't know the difference between what is real and what I have been pretending. I have spent my whole life building something, and I cannot tell if any of it is mine.'

script.visual: The Guru does not respond. He listens. He looks at the disciple the way a surgeon looks at an X-ray. Then — a small smile. Not comforting. Recognition.
script.voiceover: The old man looked at him for a long time. Then he smiled — the smile of someone recognizing a guest they had been expecting.

script.visual: Hold on the smile. Cut to black. Single word.
script.voiceover: 'Good,' the Guru said. 'You have brought the only thing worth bringing.'

cta: Scene two. What would you say if the question you had rehearsed for years dissolved on your tongue?
caption:
The disciple had a speech. Clean paragraphs. Business-case language for a longing he could not name. Then the Guru asked one word and the speech collapsed.

What came out instead was the real question — six of them, actually, and they are the six questions that open the Vivekachudamani.

The teaching does not begin with answers. It begins with the moment your rehearsed life falls apart in your mouth.

praśna
music_suggestion: Silence under the rehearsal montage — just ambient room tone. A single held violin note, barely audible, enters at the confession and rises almost imperceptibly through the six 'I don't know' lines. Silence again for the smile. No drums. No bells. No singing bowls.
casting_notes: Two characters: The Seeker (mid-30s to 40s, well-dressed, cracked open — the crack should be visible without being theatrical; shot in firelight or single-source window light) and The Guru (older, economy of motion, speaks three sentences total across the reel). The Guru must be able to hold a long silence on camera without blinking or shifting — casting is about stillness, not looks. Alternative: shot entirely in firelit close-ups so actors can be non-professional.
